questions.scenario: Failure rate is rising quickly, the previous revision is healthy, and rollback requires only traffic routing. What decision best fits?

options.text: Roll back quickly and verify recovery.
Fix forward later while users continue failing.
Wait for more failures to improve confidence.
Disable alerts so the team can focus.

questions.explanation: Clear user harm plus a known healthy rollback target favors rollback.

questions.scenario: A release introduced a small text bug with no checkout impact, and a tested one-line fix is ready. Rollback would undo unrelated safe changes. What decision may fit?

options.text: Fix forward with the narrow tested correction and keep the watch window.
Roll back every release change immediately.
Ignore the bug because it has no checkout impact.
Create a new resource group to isolate the text bug.

questions.explanation: Fix forward can be reasonable when the issue is understood, low impact, and the fix is narrow and verifiable.

questions.scenario: Which facts should influence rollback vs fix-forward choice?

options.text: Whether the rollback target is known and healthy.
Whether data or config changes make rollback risky.
Which option sounds faster without evidence.

questions.explanation: The decision should use impact, recovery target, and side effects. Speed without evidence is not enough.
